title: Module selection guidance
replyText:
Thank you for your enquiry regarding module selection.

Please refer to your programme handbook for the full list of available modules for your year of study. If you have specific questions about module content, please contact the relevant module leader directly.

To make changes to your module selection, please complete the Module Change Request form available on the student portal and submit it before the published deadline.

Kind regards

title: Module change â€” after deadline
replyText:
Thank you for your request to change modules.

Unfortunately, the deadline for module changes has now passed. Late changes can only be considered in exceptional circumstances and require approval from your Programme Director.

If you believe you have exceptional circumstances, please submit a written request to your Programme Director explaining your reasons, and we will advise you of the outcome.

Kind regards

title: Module reading list request
replyText:
Thank you for your enquiry about reading lists.

Reading lists for all modules are available on the university's online reading list system, accessible via the library website. Your module leader may also share additional recommended readings on the VLE.

If you are unable to locate the reading list for a specific module, please contact the module leader directly.

Kind regards

title: Academic advisor meeting â€” how to book
replyText:
Thank you for getting in touch.

To book a meeting with your academic advisor, please use the online booking system available on the student portal under 'Academic Support'. Your advisor's available slots are updated weekly.

If no suitable slots are available, please email your advisor directly to arrange an alternative time.

Kind regards
keywords: advisor, meeting, book, appointment, personal tutor

title: Request to change academic advisor
replyText:
Thank you for your request to change your academic advisor.

We understand that the advisor relationship is important. Please submit your request in writing to the Programme Director, briefly outlining your reasons. All requests are treated confidentially.

We will aim to process your request within 10 working days.

Kind regards
keywords: advisor, change, swap, personal tutor, reassign

title: Assessment submission confirmation
replyText:
Thank you for your enquiry about your assessment submission.

You should have received an automated confirmation email upon successful submission via the VLE. If you did not receive this, please check your spam folder.

If you are still concerned, please provide the module code and assessment title and we will check with the module team.

Kind regards

title: Assessment deferral â€” approved
replyText:
I am writing to confirm that your request to defer your assessment has been approved.

Your new submission deadline is noted on your student record. Please check the student portal for the updated date. All other assessment requirements remain unchanged.

If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to get in touch.

Kind regards

title: Resit assessment â€” information
replyText:
Thank you for your enquiry about resit assessments.

Resit dates and details are published on the student portal under 'Assessment'. Please ensure you are registered for any resit assessments by the published deadline. Resit marks are normally capped at the pass mark unless you have approved mitigating circumstances.

Please refer to the Assessment Regulations for full details.

Kind regards

title: Assessment feedback â€” when available
replyText:
Thank you for your enquiry about assessment feedback.

Feedback is normally returned within 20 working days of the submission deadline. You will be notified via the VLE when your feedback is available.

If this period has passed and you have not received feedback, please contact the module leader directly.

Kind regards

title: Extension granted â€” 5 working days
replyText:
I am writing to confirm that your extension request has been approved.

You have been granted an extension of 5 working days. Your new submission deadline is [INSERT DATE]. Please ensure you submit your work by this date via the VLE.

Please note that no further extensions can be granted for this assessment without a formal mitigating circumstances application.

Kind regards
keywords: extension, deadline, granted, approved, extra time

title: Extension request â€” declined
replyText:
Thank you for your extension request.

Unfortunately, we are unable to approve your request as the grounds provided do not meet the criteria for an extension. The original deadline remains in place.

If you are experiencing ongoing difficulties, you may wish to consider submitting a mitigating circumstances application. Details are available on the student portal.

Kind regards

title: Mitigating circumstances â€” application guidance
replyText:
Thank you for your enquiry about mitigating circumstances.

To submit a claim, please complete the Mitigating Circumstances Form available on the student portal. You will need to provide:
- A description of your circumstances
- The assessments affected
- Supporting evidence (e.g., medical certificate, bereavement notice)

Claims should normally be submitted within 5 working days of the affected assessment deadline.

Kind regards

title: Mitigating circumstances â€” approved
replyText:
I am writing to inform you that your mitigating circumstances claim has been considered and approved.

The panel has agreed the following outcome: [INSERT OUTCOME â€” e.g., deferral to next assessment period / uncapped resit / extension].

Please check the student portal for any updated deadlines. If you have questions about the outcome, please contact the Mitigating Circumstances team.

Kind regards

title: Mitigating circumstances â€” not approved
replyText:
I am writing to inform you that your mitigating circumstances claim has been considered but was not approved on this occasion.

The panel determined that the evidence provided was insufficient to support the claim. You have the right to appeal this decision within 10 working days. Details of the appeals process are available on the student portal.

Kind regards

title: Absence recorded â€” no action needed
replyText:
Thank you for notifying us of your absence.

Your absence has been recorded. As this is within the permitted threshold, no further action is required at this time. Please ensure you catch up on any missed material via the VLE.

If your absence is likely to be prolonged, please contact us again so we can provide appropriate support.

Kind regards

title: Attendance concern â€” below threshold
replyText:
We are writing to let you know that your attendance has fallen below the expected threshold of 80%.

Regular attendance is important for your academic progress and may affect your eligibility for certain support services. We would like to arrange a meeting to discuss any difficulties you may be experiencing and how we can help.

Please contact your academic advisor or reply to this email to arrange a convenient time.

Kind regards
keywords: attendance, low, threshold, warning, concern, engagement

title: Timetable â€” where to find
replyText:
Thank you for your timetable enquiry.

Your personalised timetable is available on the student portal under 'My Timetable'. Timetables are updated regularly, so please check before each week.

If you notice any clashes or errors, please report them to the timetabling team via the online form on the student portal.

Kind regards

title: Room change notification
replyText:
Please note that the following session has been moved to a different room:

- Module: [MODULE NAME]
- Date: [DATE]
- New room: [ROOM]

Please check your timetable on the student portal for the most up-to-date information. We apologise for any inconvenience.

Kind regards

title: Password reset instructions
replyText:
Thank you for contacting us about your login issue.

You can reset your password using the self-service portal at [LINK]. You will need your student ID number and registered email address.

If you continue to experience difficulties after resetting your password, please contact the IT Service Desk directly on [PHONE] or visit them in [LOCATION].

Kind regards
keywords: password, reset, login, locked, access, account

title: VLE access â€” module not showing
replyText:
Thank you for reporting that a module is not appearing on your VLE dashboard.

This is usually due to a delay in enrolment synchronisation. Please allow 24-48 hours for the system to update. If the module still does not appear after this time, please contact the IT Service Desk with your student ID and the module code.

Kind regards
keywords: VLE, module, missing, access, enrolment, Blackboard, Moodle

title: Student email â€” setup guide
replyText:
Thank you for your enquiry about setting up your student email.

Your university email can be accessed via Outlook on the web at [LINK]. To set it up on your phone or desktop, use the following settings:
- Server: outlook.office365.com
- Username: your full university email address
- Password: your university password

Step-by-step guides are available on the IT pages of the student portal.

Kind regards
keywords: email, setup, Outlook, configure, phone, mobile

title: Tuition fee balance â€” how to check
replyText:
Thank you for your enquiry about your tuition fee balance.

You can view your current fee balance and any payments received on the student portal under 'Finance'. If you have a student loan, payments from the Student Loans Company are typically received in three instalments across the academic year.

For detailed queries about your account, please contact the Finance Office at [EMAIL].

Kind regards
keywords: fees, tuition, balance, payment, finance, SLC

title: Fee instalment plan â€” request
replyText:
Thank you for your enquiry about paying your fees by instalment.

We do offer instalment plans for self-funded students. To apply, please complete the Instalment Plan Request form available on the student portal under 'Finance'. You will need to set up a direct debit as part of the arrangement.

Please note that applications must be submitted before the first payment deadline.

Kind regards
keywords: instalment, payment plan, fees, direct debit, self-funded

title: General enquiry â€” acknowledgement
replyText:
Thank you for your enquiry.

We have received your message and will respond as soon as possible. Most enquiries are answered within 3-5 working days. If your matter is urgent, please call us on [PHONE] or visit the Student Hub in [LOCATION].

Kind regards

title: Referral to specialist service
replyText:
Thank you for getting in touch.

Based on your enquiry, we think you would benefit from speaking with our [SERVICE NAME] team, who are best placed to assist you.

You can contact them at [EMAIL/PHONE] or book an appointment via the student portal. We have also forwarded your details to them so they are aware of your enquiry.

Kind regards
keywords: referral, redirect, specialist, wellbeing, counselling, careers

title: How do I access my results
replyText: Decisions of the Boards of Examiners about results are available to students from Banner Self Service. You should log into the Banner Self Service login page after the date and time listed on the Pass Lists (sharepoint.com).

title: How will I know if I need to take resit examinations
replyText:
Students who are required to take assessments will be able to identify these when viewing results in Banner Self Service. If stated â€œResit(s) Requiredâ€ or â€œApproved concession to retake assessments as first attemptâ€ then you will be required to take resit assessments. These will usually be sat in the August re-sit examination period.

Undergraduate studentsâ€™ modules must be passed at 40% to obtain credits, a module mark below this is considered a fail.  Students should check if/how many credits they are permitted to resit. You will be informed if you are required to resit modules, and which component/s, via your pass list, along with any concessions or deferrals noted.

Students will be issued resit coursework shortly after the release of marks.  Resit examinations take place for most students in August each year.  All students must make themselves available at this time.  If you are required to resit components at a second attempt for modules you failed, the overall module mark will be capped at the pass mark.

I have failed my overall module but passed one of the components.  Will I need to sit all the components?

When you log in to Banner Self-Service to view your results and progression outcome, it should tell you which components you need to re-sit. Normally the short answer to your question is no. The general rule is that you resit failed components of failed modules.

If you are required to resit any assessments, please note that modules are then capped at the overall module mark (not the component mark)

title: Progression Regulations
replyText: For detailed information please see: Progression flowchart In summary: Level 1: A student must achieve a pass of at least 40% in each module taken at Level 1 (to a total of 120 credits) to be allowed to progress to Level 2. Level 2: A student must achieve a pass of at least 40% in each module taken at Level 2 (to a total of 120 credits) to be allowed to progress to Level 3 in the programme of study for the Honours degree for which he/she is registered. Level 3. To be awarded a Bachelorâ€™s degree with honours, you must pass all 120 credits, getting a mark of 40 or more in each module which you take. At least 90 of these credits must be in Level 3 modules. Modules up to the value of 40 credits taken at Level 3, or modules up to the value of 20 credits at Level 3 and 20 credits at Level 2, may be passed by Compensation If you do not meet the requirements to be awarded a Bachelor's degree with honours, you may be eligible for a Bachelorâ€™s degree without honours (an ordinary degree). To be awarded an ordinary degree, you must pass at least 60 credits of Level 3 modules with a mark of 40 or above, and have passed at least 300 credits in total from study at Levels 1, 2 and 3. At Level 3 there is no opportunity to resit failed examinations and/or resubmit failed assignments. You can also find more about this in the Core Regulations for Undergraduate Programmes.

title: What are the resit rules for Level 1 students
replyText: Important, first please, refer to the Progression Flowchart for information relating to your own circumstances. In summary, at Level 1, you will have the chance to resit failed examinations and/or resubmit failed assignments in modules to the value of 120 credits on one occasion only; normally in the August resit period. The mark for any module which is resat will be capped at 40. If, after resitting/resubmitting, you have passed 120 credits with marks of at least 40, you may proceed to Level 2 on an honours degree. If, after resitting/resubmitting, you have passed 100 credits with marks of at least 40, you may proceed to Level 2 carrying the failed module, as long as you have not failed a module which is a pre-requisite for Level 2 study (such modules are marked with a # symbol in the regulations), or which is a pre-requisite for a core module at Level 2 specifically (pre-requisites are listed within module descriptions). If, after resitting/resubmitting, you have either passed fewer than 100 credits with marks of at least 40, or you have failed a module which is a pre-requisite for Level 2 study (such modules are marked with a # symbol in the regulations), you will be required to leave the University. You may be eligible for an exit qualification. Please refer to the Core Regulations for Undergraduate Programmes for further guidance

title: What are the resit rules for Level 2 students
replyText: Important, first please, refer to the Progression Flowchart for information relating to your own circumstances. In summary, if a student gains an overall mark of less than 40% in any one or more modules to the value of not more than 60 credits, he/she will be required to resit the failed assessment element(s) of any failed assessment component(s) (e.g. where the examinations have been failed overall but the coursework passed, a student must resit the failed examination paper(s) and those only If you are on a Bachelorâ€™s degree programme and, after resitting/resubmitting, you pass 120 credits with marks of at least 40, you may proceed to Level 3 on an/a Bachelorâ€™s honours degree. If, after resitting/resubmitting, you have passed 100 credits with marks of at least 40, you may proceed to Level 3 on an ordinary degree, as long as you have not failed a module which is a pre-requisite for Level 3 study (such modules are marked with a # symbol in the regulations). If, after resitting/resubmitting, you have either passed fewer than 100 credits with marks of at least 40, or as you have failed a module which is a pre-requisite for Level 3 study (such modules are marked with a # symbol in the regulations), you will be required to leave the University. You may be eligible for an exit qualification. You can also read the Core Regulations for Undergraduate Programmes for detailed information. There are no resit opportunities at Level 3. Please, refer to the Progression Flowchart for more information.

title: What are Exit qualifications
replyText: Important, please refer to the Progression Flowchart for more information on exit qualifications. In summary, should you choose, or be required, to leave your degree before completion, or should you fail to gain the credits required for an honours or ordinary degree, you may be eligible for one of the following exit awards: Certificate of Higher Education: if you have passed 120 credits at Level 1 (including up to 20 credits passed by Compensation) Diploma of Higher Education: if you have passed 240 credits in total (including at least 90 Level 2 credits; and including up to 40 credits passed by Compensation)

title: I am level 3 student and one or two of my module marks were below the pass mark.  Why I have not been given the opportunity to resit these
replyText: There are no resit opportunities at Level 3. Please, refer to the Progression Flowchart for more information. There are no resits at Level 3 (or Level 4). The university regulations allow a certain amount of module failure to be â€œcompensatedâ€. So, even if one or two of your Level 3 module marks are below 40, if they are at least 30 then it is likely that they will have been compensated, and in this case the regulations still allow you to be awarded an Honours degree.

title: What is an â€˜Ordinaryâ€™ degree
replyText: All Business School degrees studied at undergraduate level are Honours degrees. A Bachelorâ€™s degree with Honours (BA/BSc) usually lasts 3 years and is gained by achieving 360 credits. Therefore, you must pass all modules to be awarded an Honours degree (please note: a limited number of modules may also be passed by compensation within Core Regulations for Undergraduate Programmes Students who have not achieved the required number of credits for an Honours degree may be eligible for the award of an Ordinary degree. Students will register for the same number of modules per year and take the same curriculum. In some circumstances, students may return to the Honours route in subsequent years.

title: I am required to withdraw but why canâ€™t they I do a further resit
replyText: Unfortunately, this means that you have not met this requirement and so must be withdrawn from the University. I realise that this is disappointing, but the regulations do not permit your request. The decision of the Board of Examiners is final and as you have not been granted any further resits, you are required to withdraw from the University. Please refer to the Core Regulations for Undergraduate Programmes for further guidance.

title: I am required to withdraw but can I repeat the year instead
replyText: Students are only permitted to repeat the year in very exceptional circumstances, typically where students have missed a term or more of study. Students who have completed the year and taken assessments are not permitted to repeat. The regulations do not permit your request. Please refer to the Core Regulations for Undergraduate Programmes for further guidance.

title: I have failed a module with # symbol, why am I required to withdraw
replyText: In order to proceed to the next year, students need to pass all modules which are pre-requisites for studying core modules in the following years of their programme (such modules are marked with a # symbol in the regulations).  Students who have failed a #module are required to withdraw.

title: Can I have my exam remarked
replyText: All summative work is marked in accordance with university guidelines which includes double marking and external moderation as an important means of assuring the quality of the assessment process. Marks are awarded in line with the grade descriptors and results/classifications are confirmed by the Board of Examiners. Summative assignments and examinations go through both internal University and external moderation, in line with standard University regulations with an External Examiner. Due to this rigorous process, there is no mechanism for students' work to be remarked or looked at again. We also cannot comment on individual scripts but can confirm that the examination passed internal and external inspection and was deemed an appropriate test of the module learning outcomes. The marking has similarly progressed through due process with the marks ratified at the recent examination board. We do not release model answers and/or marking schemes for examinations. There is no provision for scripts to be reviewed and marks to be revised, however, you can of course appeal if you consider you have legitimate grounds. You can find information on the appeals process here: https://www.dur.ac.uk/cla/appeals/.

title: The grounds for academic appeal are limited to the following
replyText:
(a) that you were adversely affected by illness or other relevant factors, of which you were previously unaware or which for a good cause you were unable to disclose to the Examiners or other decision-making body or Officer

AND/OR

(b) that you have evidence that parts of the relevant documented procedure were not applied correctly or your marks were not calculated correctly and this procedural defect was significant enough to have materially affected the decision, making it unsound

AND/OR

(c) that the Board of Examiners or other decision making body or Officer took a decision which was not reasonable in all the circumstances.

title: What is meant by academic judgement
replyText: Matters of academic judgement cannot be appealed. The University defines â€œacademic judgementâ€ as the professional and scholarly knowledge and expertise which members of Durham University staff and the external examiners draw upon in reaching an academic decision. Academic judgement therefore includes, but is not restricted to, decisions about the severity of impact of mitigating circumstances on academic performance, decisions about the academic standard attained by students, marks and grades to be awarded for individual pieces of work or modules, and degrees and degree classifications to be awarded, or not. If you feel that you have been affected by Serious Adverse Circumstances that you have not declared, please provide further details and clear evidence to explain why you could not bring this matter to the Boardâ€™s attention in the required timeframe. Please refer to the University General Regulation VII â€“ Academic Appeal

title: Can I make an Academic appeal against an exam board decision
replyText: You can find information on the appeals process here: https://www.dur.ac.uk/cla/appeals/. Two things to note. Firstly, appeals must be submitted within 21 days. Secondly, note that there is no appeal against academic judgement. The appeals process flowchart links to the general regulations of the University. Academic appeals are covered by General Regulation V11 - see https://www.dur.ac.uk/university.calendar/volumei/general_regulations/. You are strongly recommended to take advice from your College Student Support Officer.

title: Overall ranking
replyText: We do not publish the average marks across programmes or provide students with their individual student ranking within their cohort.

title: Can you convert my Durham mark into a GPA
replyText: No. The University does not operate a GPA ranking and does not have an agreed GPA scale. The Universityâ€™s classification arrangements are designed to be consistent with the QAA Framework for Higher Education Qualifications (England, Wales and Northern Ireland). We can provide a letter to confirm that a GPA is not available.

title: Will One Bad Mark Seriously Affect My Degree Classification
replyText: It depends very much on the nature of the work. A poor final year Dissertation mark may have a substantial impact, since the Dissertation is a double module and counts as 20% of the total degree marks. However, one essay counting towards the assessment of a single module will have less of an impact. If the poor mark has resulted from adverse personal circumstances over the examination period and these have been reported in the correct way via a Serious Adverse Circumstances online form, then the Board of Examiners will consider those according to University procedures. Information can also be found in the Student Information Hub, including where to find full details of the Universityâ€™s policy on serious adverse circumstances. If the poor mark is for coursework, then the circumstances affecting this should have been reported at the time so that mitigation (e.g., a coursework extension) will already have been given. Where, for good reason, such circumstances were not reported at the time of occurrence they may be reported on the Serious Adverse Circumstances form, but it is expected that very rarely should this be necessary.

title: I submitted a Serious Adverse Circumstances Form, but it seems to have had no effect. Why
replyText: You may wish to see the detailed guidance about SACs and the possible outcomes here: Serious Adverse Circumstances Online Process (sharepoint.com). The Scrutiny Sub-Committee considers all serious adverse circumstances evidence submitted by students and, for each submission, grade the impact of the serious adverse circumstances reported on the assessment of the student concerned. in accordance with the following impact scale: Submitting a Serious Adverse Circumstances (SAC) form does not allow the Board of Examiners (BoE) to alter your marks nor is it guaranteed to change any outcomes. Guidance on submitting SAC forms, and an indication of the types of 'discretion' that the BoE can exercise, are given in the notes that accompany the SAC form. Information can also be found in the Student Information Hub, including where to find full details of the Universityâ€™s policy on serious adverse circumstances. There is no process by which departments routinely write to students regarding their SAC submissions. Please be assured that the Board of Examiners carefully consider Serious Adverse Circumstances that students submit and in light of the reason/evidence provided, along with any previous mitigation a decision will be made as to the severity grading. Further Information on what a SAC does and does not do. Final Year Students (UG) If you are a Final Year student and you have received a SAC grade, then this will be taken into account when the Board of Examiners is coming to a decision about your degree classification. Further information about the Board of Examinersâ€™ use of discretion can be found in Section 6 of the L&T Handbook here. Non-Final Year Students If you are NOT in your Final Year then SAC grades will be considered in relation to progression or degree classification in later years. If you are in your Foundation Year or your First Year of study of an undergraduate degree, and you pass all of your exams, your SAC grade will be recorded by the Board of Examiners but no further action will be taken in relation to your programme of study. This is because Foundation Year/First Year marks do not count towards your degree classification. If you are in your Foundation Year or your First Year of study of an undergraduate degree, and you fail one or more of your exams, your SAC grade will be acknowledged by the Board of Examiners and a decision will be made as to whether or not a further first attempt will be offered. If you are in your Second Year of study of an undergraduate degree (or third of a four year course), and you pass all of your exams, your SAC grade will be acknowledged by the Board of Examiners this year but no action will be taken at this time. The SAC grade will be carried over and considered at the Board of Examiners of your Final Year when your degree is being classified. If you are in your Second Year of study of an undergraduate degree (or third of a four year course), and you have failed an exam and you have received a SAC grade then the Board of Examiners will decide what appropriate action should be taken (if any). A Board could, for example, award a student a further first attempt rather than a re-sit opportunity. If your SAC Form relates to circumstances impacting on exams taken during the August examination period, then you need to be aware that the â€˜next available opportunityâ€™ is normally May/June of the following year. Students who are required to take exams (either as first attempts OR resits) in May/June of the following year will be required to take a â€˜Year out of Studyâ€™. A SAC grade cannot raise your marks.

title: What If My Mark Is Much Lower Than Expected
replyText:
Undergraduates understandably are often disappointed or upset when they feel that a mark is lower than they desired or expected. Frequently they may feel that they put a lot of effort into the work, and although such effort may sometimes be reflected in the quality of the work produced, this is unfortunately not always the case. We cannot quantify effort, and this is not directly reflected in the mark given.

Marks are the outcome of markers' academic judgement, and your personal feelings are not a basis for challenging this. In other words, marks are not negotiable just because they are disappointing. We trust the academic judgement of our markers and the robustness of our marking procedures, and hence re-marking of work will not be undertaken except in cases where errors or procedural irregularity are found to have occurred.

We do, however, want to ensure that you understand the feedback that you are given on your work and can rationalise the mark given with the work in question. There are several things that you should consider (going as far down the list as is needed to gain satisfaction):

Look very carefully at the feedback. Markers should give feedback that explains, in detail if appropriate, why a particular mark was awarded. Usually on reflection, students can see that a low mark does mean that they were seriously wrong in some way, e.g., by misreading a question or omitting an important dimension in their submission

Look carefully at the assignment and the feedback in relation to the assessment criteria and assessment descriptors, as these can often help to explain the positioning of your work on the mark scale

Request a brief meeting with the module leader seeking any additional advice and feedback they can give you. They might also direct you to the tutor / marker in question, but you should go to them first

If there is evidence of some procedural irregularity or error, then the matter will be discussed with the Chair of the Board of Examiners and the markers and appropriate action taken

title: What are discretion decisions
replyText:
Where a studentâ€™s arithmetic mean (AMW mark) falls no more than 2% below a classification boundary, the board of examiners must consider whether to award the higher classification by the exercise of discretion. Board of Examiners may only consider exercising discretion to raise a classification for a student whose arithmetic mean falls outside of this 2% range where there are personal mitigating circumstances, or a combination of personal mitigating circumstances and academic factors, that warrant such consideration.

The Board of Examiners has the power to exercise discretion to take account of particular circumstances if it considers it right to do so in the proper discharge of its duties. Such circumstances shall include, but not be restricted to, personal mitigating circumstances. Boards of Examiners may also take into account academic factors such as the overall pattern of marks gained by a student and the relative significance of various modules, or additional placement years, for the learning outcomes of the programme. The views of an External Examiner must be particularly influential in the case of disagreement within the Board of Examiners on the mark to be awarded for a particular unit of assessment, or on the final level of award to be derived from the array of marks of a particular student.

There are a number of factors the exam board takes into account when considering whether to classify a degree in a higher classification by using its discretion, such as the performance in the dissertation, the proportion of credits in higher classifications, whether a student improved their performance over the time of their degree, whether a student suffered from serious adverse circumstances.

If your average was in the 2% discretion range the exam board considered explicitly and carefully whether your degree could be classified.

title: Undergraduate classification conventions
How is my final degree classification calculated
replyText: For detailed information on how degrees are classified please see the Student Information Hub: Examinations and Assessment (sharepoint.com) The University records module marks as integers, but AMW marks are recorded to two decimal places and are not rounded up or down. So, for example, if your final AMW mark works out to be 59.99 then this does not get rounded up to 60.

title: I have completed my final year and received my final results.  Does my official degree classification relate just to my final yearâ€™s mark or to my overall degree
replyText: For final year students, the result which you can see when you log into Banner Self-Service is your overall final degree classification (i.e., not just your final year).
keywords: results, degree, 2:2, 2:1, class, first, marks, banner

title: Subject Access Request for exam feedback
replyText:
It is not University policy to supply individual feedback to students for examinations. Generic examination feedback will be made available to all students via the relevant Learn Ultra module site and is the responsibility of Module Leaders to publish. Students should refer to this for an understanding of expectations.

Examination script comments written in Feedback Studio by markers are not intended to be utilised by students, rather this text is concerned with the marking process and written by the markers for quality assurance purposes. As such, it is not specifically directed at students on how they can improve their work. Students who fail a module are permitted a feedback session with the relevant Module Leader to discuss areas for improvement in the failed assessment(s).

title: Late Submissions of Exams
replyText: As per regulations, exams submitted late are not accepted and are classed as a non-submission.

title: I have some resit exams in August. Will I get a feedback session with the Module leader
replyText: Students who fail a module are entitled to receive feedback to help them prepare for their resit.  This feedback may be provided in group feedback sessions or in a one-to-one meeting with the student using a medium that will suit i.e. Skype/Teams. Also please remember that if you have to resit an exam this will be in the same format as the exam was in the main exam period.

title: What is the deadline for my resit summative assignment(s)
replyText: Friday 2nd August 2024 12 pm.  You will receive instructions via e-mail at least 4 weeks prior to the deadline.

title: What are the dates for resit exams
replyText: Resit Examination Period: Monday 5th August to Friday 16th August 2024 inclusive. All undergraduate students must keep these times free and consult the final copies of the examination timetables before making any other commitments.

title: What is the format of my resit exams in August 2024
replyText: Examinations for modules in August 2024 will generally be the same format as the May 2024 examination.  Therefore, if the exam in May is on-campus, the August resit will be on-campus.  You should check with your department for the specific arrangements for your resit examinations.
